metadata.title: The Cowork Access Ladder: Lowest Rung That Works

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I keep reaching for a Drive connector out of habit, and the six-rung ladder makes me want to justify each rung. Take a real case: I need Claude to summarize three project notes plus cross-reference one client contract. Help me: (1) name which rung each of those files actually requires and why uploaded task files beats a connector here; (2) show exactly what the connector would expose beyond my three-to-four files; (3) give me a one-line rule I can say out loud before every Cowork session to force myself onto the lowest rung that completes the task.

props.command: I keep reaching for a Drive connector out of habit, and the six-rung ladder makes me want to justify each rung. Take a real case: I need Claude to summarize three project notes plus cross-reference one client contract. Help me: (1) name which rung each of those files actually requires and why uploaded task files beats a connector here; (2) show exactly what the connector would expose beyond my three-to-four files; (3) give me a one-line rule I can say out loud before every Cowork session to force myself onto the lowest rung that completes the task.
props.segment: The Cowork Access Ladder: Lowest Rung

beats.narration_text: She connected her entire Google Drive to Cowork to summarize three project notes. Claude read the three notes — and the payroll folder, the personnel records, and the draft emails in her sent box. Nothing bad happened. But everything in the Drive was source material. The task needed three files. The access ladder was connected at the wrong rung. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, summarize three project notes

props.command: summarize the three project notes in my Drive

beats.narration_text: Cowork's access ladder has six rungs. Chat only — no file access. Uploaded task files — you attach exactly what you want. Local folder — a designated workspace on your machine. Cloud connector — structured access to a service, often your whole account. Browser — page interaction, with prompt-injection risk. Computer use — Cowork sees your screen. Each rung up adds capability and exposure.
beats.new_visual_element: Six-rung ladder table — Chat / Upload / Folder / Connector / Browser / Computer use

beats.narration_text: Connectors are the common mistake. Connecting a cloud drive usually means connecting the entire account — not the folder you had in mind. The connector does not know you meant three files. It has access to everything the service exposes. When a task needs three files and the connector exposes five hundred, that is a scope error, not a setup.
beats.new_visual_element: Connector scope gap — task=3 files vs. connector=whole account

beats.narration_text: The safest rung for document-heavy one-off tasks is uploaded task files. You choose the files. You attach them. Nothing else enters the session. When the session ends, the access ends. A task that can run on three uploaded files does not need a connector. The upload rung is not a limitation — it is the correct scope for the task.
beats.new_visual_element: Upload rung — three files in, session ends, access ends

beats.narration_text: The principle is one sentence: prefer the lowest rung that completes the task. A task done with three uploaded files does not need a connector. A connector task does not need browser access. A browser task does not need computer use. Each rung down toward computer use adds brittleness, prompt-injection exposure, and less predictable behavior. Start at the bottom. Climb only when the rung below fails.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — rung rule: lowest that works / climb only when below fails

beats.narration_text: Before your next Cowork task, audit the rung you are about to use. Paste this.

props.command: Before I start this Cowork task, help me choose the right access rung. My task is: [describe task]. The source material is: [describe files or services]. Tell me: (1) which rung is the minimum needed, (2) whether my planned access is wider than the task requires, (3) what I should attach or restrict to stay on the minimum rung.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The principle is one sentence: prefer the lowest rung that completes the task. A task done with three uploaded files does not need a connector. A connector task does not need browser access. A browser task does not need computer use. Each rung down toward computer use adds brittleness, prompt-injection exposure, and less predictable behavior. Start at the bottom. Climb only when the rung below fails. Before your next Cowork task, audit the rung you are about to use. Paste this.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
